questions.question: Explain the slow and fast pointer technique for finding the middle element of a linked list. What is its time and space complexity?
keyPoints: Use two pointers: slow moves one node, fast moves two nodes at a time
When fast reaches end, slow is at middle node
Time complexity: O(n) - single traversal
Space complexity: O(1) - only two pointer variables used
Handles both even and odd length lists

questions.question: Describe Floyd's cycle detection algorithm. How does it detect a cycle and find the starting node of the cycle?
keyPoints: Uses two pointers: slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps)
If they meet, cycle exists
To find start: reset one pointer to head, keep other at meeting point
Move both one step at a time - they meet at cycle start
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

questions.question: Write an algorithm to find the nth node from the end of a linked list in a single traversal. Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Use two pointers separated by n nodes
Move both until fast reaches NULL
When fast is NULL, slow points to nth from end
Example: list 1->2->3->4->5, n=2, result=4
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

questions.question: How do you merge two sorted linked lists? Write the algorithm and state its time complexity.
keyPoints: Create a dummy node for result list
Compare heads of both lists
Pick smaller node and attach to result
Move pointer of the list from which node was taken
Repeat until one list exhausts, attach remaining
Time: O(m+n), Space: O(1)

questions.question: Explain the algorithm to check whether a linked list is a palindrome. What are the steps involved?
keyPoints: Find middle using slow/fast pointers
Reverse the second half of the list
Compare first half with reversed second half
If all nodes match, list is palindrome
Restore the list by reversing again (optional)

questions.question: Describe the process of cloning a linked list with random pointers using a hash map approach.
keyPoints: First pass: create new nodes for all original nodes
Store mapping of original node to clone node in hash map
Second pass: traverse again, set next pointer from map
Set random pointer using map to find corresponding clone
Time: O(n), Space: O(n) for hash map

questions.question: How do you rotate a linked list by k positions to the right? Explain the steps involved.
keyPoints: If k is 0 or equals length, no rotation needed
Connect last node to head forming a circular list
Calculate new head position: (n - k % n)th node
Make that node the new head
Break the connection at that point

questions.question: Write a complete algorithm to reverse a linked list iteratively. Explain each step with a diagram showing the state of pointers at each iteration.
keyPoints: Initialize three pointers: prev as NULL, curr as head, next as NULL
For each iteration: save next node, reverse current's pointer to prev, move prev and curr forward
Continue until curr becomes NULL
Finally, set head to prev (new head)
Provide diagram showing transformation from 1->2->3->NULL to NULL<-1<-2<-3
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

questions.question: Explain how to find the intersection point of two linked lists. Discuss both the hash-based approach and the two-pointer approach with their time and space complexities.
keyPoints: Hash approach: traverse first list, store nodes in hash set, traverse second list, first common node is intersection
Two-pointer: find lengths, advance longer list by difference, then traverse together until nodes match
Hash approach: Time O(m+n), Space O(m)
Two-pointer: Time O(m+n), Space O(1)
Explain with example where lists converge

questions.question: Describe the algorithm to add two numbers represented as linked lists. Show how reverse operation helps in this process.
keyPoints: Each digit stored as a node in reverse order (least significant digit first)
Reverse both input lists
Traverse both lists simultaneously, add corresponding digits with carry
Create new nodes for sum digits
After traversal, if carry remains, create new node
Reverse the result list to get final answer
Time: O(max(m,n)), Space: O(max(m,n))

questions.question: Write an algorithm to remove duplicates from an unsorted linked list. Discuss both hash-based and without-hash approaches with their trade-offs.
keyPoints: Hash approach: use hash set to track seen values, O(n) time, O(n) space
Without hash (sorting): sort the list first O(n log n), then remove consecutive duplicates O(n)
Without hash (two loops): compare each node with every other node O(n^2) time, O(1) space
Choose based on space availability
Handle edge cases: empty list, single node, all duplicates
